articles.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting When You Suddenly Have a Brokerage Account
articles.metaDescription: Learn how to use tax-loss harvesting in your new brokerage account to offset capital gains and reduce your taxable income by up to $3,000 after a divorce.
articles.body:
Tax-loss harvesting uses investment losses to reduce your taxable income. You can offset investment gains and up to $3,000 of your ordinary income annually. Be careful of the wash-sale rule, which prevents you from claiming a loss if you buy a similar investment within 30 days.

Look, The divorce is final. Suddenly, you have a brokerage account, and it's all yours. It might be a portfolio you never paid much attention to, a collection of assets your ex managed, or the result of a split account. Look, it doesn't matter how you got it. What matters is what you do now. Inside that account are winners and losers. The winners are great. The losers? They can be useful. This is where tax-loss harvesting comes in. It sounds complex, but it’s a practical tool. You’re using the investment losses to lower your tax bill. It’s a legal, strategic move that can save you real money, especially in the years following a major life change. Let me be straight with you, this is one of those financial housekeeping tasks that pays off. You don't need to be a Wall Street expert to get it done.

The Core Idea: Turning Losses into Tax Savings

Here's the thing. The government lets you use your investment losses to your advantage. When you sell an investment for less than you paid for it, you have a capital loss. This loss can be used to cancel out capital gains. If you sold some stocks for a $10,000 profit this year, but also sold others for a $10,000 loss, they cancel each other out. Your net capital gain is zero. You pay no tax on that gain. It’s a simple concept. You balance the scales.

But what if you have more losses than gains? This is where it gets even better. After you’ve offset all your capital gains for the year, you can use up to $3,000 of your remaining capital losses to reduce your ordinary income. This is the income from your job, your business, or other sources. It’s taxed at a higher rate than long-term capital gains, so this deduction is extra valuable. If you have more than $3,000 in net losses, you can’t use it all at once. The remaining amount carries forward to future tax years. You can use it to offset future gains or take that $3,000 deduction each year until the losses are used up. This is a huge benefit when you're trying to get your finances back on track. For anyone navigating a new financial reality, learning about a one-income budget template can provide a solid foundation for managing these new variables.

Think of it as a small silver lining. Nobody likes seeing red in their investment account. But if the losses are there, you might as well put them to work. It requires a bit of planning, especially toward the end of the year. You need to look at your portfolio, identify the losing positions, and decide if selling makes sense for your overall investment strategy. Don't just sell things without a plan. The goal is to reduce your tax burden, not to wreck your long-term investment goals. For a deeper get into the rules, the IRS provides detailed guidance. You can review IRS topic 701, sale of home for an example of how specific tax rules are structured, though it's for real estate.

The Wash-Sale Rule: A Trap You Must Avoid

Stop overthinking this, but don't ignore the details. The most important rule in tax-loss harvesting is the wash-sale rule. The IRS created this rule to stop people from gaming the system. You can't sell a stock to claim the loss and then immediately buy it back. That would be too easy. A wash sale occurs if you sell a security at a loss and then buy the same or a "substantially identical" security within 30 days before or after the sale. This gives you a 61-day window to watch out for. If you trigger the wash-sale rule, the IRS disallows your capital loss for tax purposes. You can't use it to offset gains or income. The disallowed loss is instead added to the cost basis of the new investment you bought. This means you'll get the tax benefit eventually when you sell the new position, but it defeats the purpose of harvesting the loss now.

What does "substantially identical" mean? It’s not always perfectly clear. Selling shares of one company and buying them back is an obvious violation. Selling shares and buying a call option on the same stock also triggers the rule. Where it gets tricky is with things like mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Selling an S&P 500 index fund from one company and buying an S&P 500 index fund from another is almost certainly a wash sale. They track the same index and are considered substantially identical. In my own practice, I've seen people make this mistake frequently. It’s an easy one to make if you’re not paying close attention. You have to be careful. The burden is on you to track your transactions across all your accounts, including retirement accounts like IRAs. Yes, a transaction in your IRA can trigger a wash sale in your taxable brokerage account.

So how do you avoid it? The simplest way is to wait. Wait at least 31 days before you buy back the same security. Or, you can buy a similar, but not identical, investment. For example, you could sell a specific large-cap growth fund and buy a different large-cap growth fund that tracks a different index. This allows you to maintain your exposure to that asset class while still booking the tax loss. This strategy requires some research. You need to find a suitable replacement that fits your investment goals. A good reference for getting your financial documents in order is the Smead Expanding File Folder, 21 Pockets (paid link), which can help you keep track of trades and tax forms.

Smart Strategies: Lot Selection and Asset Swaps

When you sell shares, you don't have to sell all of them. And you can choose which specific shares to sell. This is called tax-lot identification. Let's say you bought shares of a company at different times and different prices. Some lots may be at a gain, while others are at a loss. If your brokerage account is set to "First-In, First-Out" (FIFO), it will automatically sell your oldest shares first. This might not be the best for tax-loss harvesting. Instead, you can use specific identification. You tell your broker exactly which lots to sell to maximize your harvested losses. This gives you precise control over the tax outcome of your sales. Most brokerage platforms let you change your cost basis accounting method online. Check your settings. Make sure it’s set to allow specific identification if you plan to do this.

Another smart move is the partner ETF swap. This is how you avoid the wash-sale rule while staying invested. You sell your losing ETF and immediately buy a similar but not identical ETF from a different provider. For example, you could sell an ETF that tracks the entire US stock market and buy another one that also tracks the US stock market but follows a slightly different index. They will perform very similarly, so you don't miss out on market movements. But because they track different indexes, they're not "substantially identical." This lets you claim the loss and keep your money working in the market. This is a common strategy used by financial advisors. It takes a little homework to find good partner ETFs, but it’s a powerful tool. Getting organized is key, and a simple tool like the Sharp EL-1801V Printing Calculator (paid link) can be surprisingly helpful for running the numbers.

This level of management is a step up from passive investing. It requires active participation. But after a divorce, taking control of your finances is a critical step. Understanding these strategies is part of that. It’s about making your assets work for you in every possible way. This is also a good time to think about the bigger picture of your financial life, including things like the 30-minute beneficiary update, which is a task that's often forgotten in the chaos of divorce.

When Tax-Loss Harvesting Is a Bad Idea

Tax-loss harvesting is not always the right move. There are situations where it can backfire or just isn’t worth the effort. The most obvious one is if you're in a very low tax bracket. If your income is low enough that you pay little to no income tax, a $3,000 deduction against your income isn’t very valuable. You might be better off holding the investment and hoping it recovers. Or, if you have no capital gains to offset, the benefit is limited to that $3,000 deduction. It’s still a benefit, but it might not be worth the transaction costs or the hassle of tracking everything.

Another thing to consider is your long-term view on the investment. If you still believe in the long-term prospects of a company or fund, selling it just for a tax loss might be a mistake. You could miss out on a significant rebound. Remember the wash-sale rule means you can’t buy it back for at least 31 days. A lot can happen in a month. You have to weigh the immediate tax savings against the potential for future growth. Sometimes, the best move is to do nothing. Don't let the tax tail wag the investment dog. Your investment strategy should always come first. Tax optimization is secondary.

Finally, be aware of how harvesting losses affects your cost basis. When you sell a security and buy a similar one, your new position has a lower cost basis. This means that when you eventually sell the new security, your taxable gain will be larger. you're deferring the tax, not eliminating it. This can be a great strategy if you expect to be in a lower tax bracket in the future. But if you expect your income to rise, you might be pushing a larger tax bill into a higher-taxed future. It’s a trade-off. For those looking to build a strong financial footing, reading a book like The Total Money Makeover (paid link) can provide a solid framework. It’s also wise to consider all aspects of your financial settlement, including the details of QDRO basics if retirement assets were part of your divorce.

articles.title: Net Worth Statement Day One: The Single Number You Need to Know
articles.metaDescription: Calculate your post-divorce financial baseline with a net worth statement. This simple tool tracks assets and liabilities to guide your financial recovery.
articles.body:
Your net worth statement is the starting point for your new financial life, a clear snapshot of where you stand today. it's a simple calculation of your assets minus your liabilities, nothing more and nothing less. Tracking this number monthly reveals the trend of your financial health, which is far more telling than any single snapshot.

Look, Look, nobody gets married expecting to calculate their net worth alone. But here you're. The divorce is final, the dust is settling, and it’s time to figure out your financial ground zero. This isn’t about judgment. It’s about clarity. Your net worth statement is that first, crucial step. It’s the single number that tells you where you're so you can start planning where you’re going. Stop overthinking this. It’s just a list and some simple math. You don’t need a finance degree to do this. You just need a quiet hour and a willingness to be honest with yourself.

What it's and What It Isn’t

A net worth statement is a simple document. It lists everything you own that has value (assets) and everything you owe (liabilities). The difference between the two is your net worth. That’s it. It’s not a measure of your worth as a person. It’s not a grade on your life choices. It’s a tool. A powerful one. In my own practice, I’ve seen how this simple document can provide a solid foundation for financial recovery. Think of it as a financial GPS. It shows your exact location. Without it, you’re just guessing at the route forward.

It isn’t a budget. A budget tracks your income and expenses, your cash flow. That’s a different tool for a different job. A net worth statement is a snapshot at a single point in time. It’s the “you're here” map for your financial journey. You’ll use it to make bigger decisions, like whether you can afford to buy a new car or when you can realistically retire. It’s also a fantastic way to track your progress over time. Seeing that number climb is a concrete sign that you’re moving in the right direction. It’s the most straightforward way to measure your financial altitude.

Let me be straight with you. This first number might be ugly. It might even be negative. That’s normal, especially after a divorce. The legal fees, the splitting of assets, the cost of setting up a new life. It all takes a toll. Don’t let a negative number discourage you. It’s just data. It’s your starting line. The only thing that matters is the direction it moves from here.

Building Your First Statement: A Line-by-Line Guide

Here’s the thing, you don’t need fancy software. A piece of paper, a spreadsheet, or a simple notes app will do. You’re going to make two lists: Assets and Liabilities. Be thorough. Be honest. No one is grading you. This is for your eyes only. Grab a coffee, put on some music, and let’s get it done.

Assets: What You Own

Start with the easy stuff. List all your cash and bank accounts. Checking, savings, money market accounts. List the exact balance on the day you’re making the statement.

Then move on to investments. This includes brokerage accounts, mutual funds, and individual stocks. Don’t forget about retirement accounts. These are often the largest assets people have. 401(k)s, 403(b)s, IRAs, Roth IRAs. If you’ve gone through the process of splitting an IRA cleanly, you know how big these numbers can be. List the current market value of each account. Log in and get the real number. No guessing.

Next, think about physical assets. Your home is the big one. You can get a rough estimate of its value from real estate websites like Zillow or Redfin. Be conservative. If you’ve recently dealt with the house-buyout math, you probably have a good idea of this number already. List any other real estate you own as well.

Cars are next. Use a site like Kelley Blue Book to find the private-party sale value. Not the trade-in value. What could you realistically sell it for today? Be honest.

Finally, list other valuable personal property. This can include jewelry, art, antiques, or high-end electronics. Again, be realistic. What you paid for something isn’t what it’s worth today. Unless you've a recent appraisal, you may need to make an educated guess. The goal is a good-faith estimate. Don’t spend weeks trying to value every single thing you own. Focus on items worth more than $1,000.

Liabilities: What You Owe

This side of the list is usually less fun, but it’s just as important. You can’t ignore it. Start with your mortgage, if you've one. List the total principal balance remaining.

Then list all other debts: car loans, student loans, and credit card balances. For credit cards, list the total statement balance, not the minimum payment. If you’re wrestling with handling joint credit-card debt, this is where you formalize what you’re responsible for. List only the debts that are legally yours now.

Don’t forget about other obligations. Do you've outstanding medical bills? A personal loan from a family member? A tax bill from the IRS? List everything. The goal here is a complete and accurate picture. You can’t make a plan if you’re working with incomplete information. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau offers great resources on credit reports and scores that can help you track down all your debts.

What to Include and What to Exclude

This part can be tricky. Let’s clear it up. You should include anything that has a clear market value and could be converted to cash. This means bank accounts, investment accounts, real estate, and vehicles. It also includes retirement accounts like 401(k)s and IRAs. The key question is: could I sell this for money? If the answer is yes, it’s probably an asset.

What should you leave out? Things with sentimental value but no real market value. Your grandmother’s wedding ring might be priceless to you, but it doesn’t belong on your net worth statement unless you’ve had it appraised and intend to sell it. Same goes for your collection of vintage t-shirts or your kid’s macaroni art. Be practical.

Also, exclude your income and expenses. Those belong on your budget. A net worth statement is about what you own and owe right now, not what you earn or spend each month. This is a snapshot, not a movie. Don’t list your salary as an asset. Don’t list your rent as a liability (unless it’s past-due). We’re focused on the balance sheet, not the income statement.

What about things like airline miles or credit card points? Generally, leave them off. They have value, but it’s not easily converted to a specific cash amount and can be devalued by the company at any time. Keep it simple. Stick to the big, clear items first. You can always add more detail later if you want.

The Monthly Cadence: Why the Trend Matters More Than the Number

Your first net worth statement is your baseline. It’s Day One. But the real power of this tool comes from tracking it over time. You need to do this every month. Yes, every month. It takes 15 minutes once you've the template set up. Set a recurring appointment on your calendar. Make it a non-negotiable part of your financial routine.

Why? Because the trend is what matters. Your net worth will fluctuate. The stock market will have good months and bad months. You’ll have unexpected expenses, like a car repair or a vet bill. That’s life. But over time, you want to see a steady, upward trend. That’s how you know you’re making progress. That’s how you know you’re rebuilding. It’s proof that your daily financial habits are paying off.

Don’t get hung up on the absolute number, especially at the beginning. It might be negative. That’s okay. A lot of people start with a negative net worth after a divorce. The goal is to make it less negative each month, and eventually, positive. This is a marathon, not a sprint. A good planner can be a big shift for staying organized. The Clever Fox Budget Planner Pro (paid link) is a great tool for this. To keep all your new financial documents in order, a Smead Expanding File Folder, 21 Pockets (paid link) is a low-tech lifesaver. And for printing out statements and forms, the Brother Compact Monochrome Laser Printer (paid link) is a reliable workhorse.

Baseline Anchoring for Decade Tracking

This first net worth statement is more than just a number. It’s an anchor. It’s the starting point for the next decade of your life. Ten years from now, you’ll look back at this document and see how far you’ve come. It will be a powerful reminder of your resilience and your ability to rebuild. It’s a story told in numbers.

Think of it as planting a tree. You don’t see the growth every day, but over the years, it becomes a towering presence. Your net worth is the same. By tracking it monthly, you’re nurturing its growth. You’re making conscious decisions that will lead to long-term financial security. This isn’t about getting rich quick. It’s about building a solid foundation for your future, one month at a time. It’s about taking control of your financial narrative.

Here's the thing. This baseline also helps you set realistic goals. Want to buy a house in five years? Your net worth statement will show you how much you need to save for a down payment and how your debt-to-asset ratio is changing. Thinking about starting a business? Your net worth is a key indicator of your financial capacity to take that risk. It grounds your dreams in reality and gives you a clear path to follow.
